text: In this video we aim to provide a thorough step-by-step technical guide of a laparoscopic splenectomy through the presentation of a case in a digitally enhanced video. We present the case of a sixty-year-old patient with no prior surgical or medical comorbidities in whom we find by accident a nodular splenic mass of unknown origin without any other suspicious lesions anywhere else. Follow-up MRI scans reveal a symptomatic growth within a three-month period, an indication for surgery.

A 12mm umbilical optical port is inserted, followed by two 5mm midline working ports and a 12mm air seal port on the left flank. The extraction site is a 5cm extension of the optical port incision. The patient is positioned in the Lloyd Davis position with the surgeon standing between the legs. The omentum is retracted above the transverse mesocolon, and the patient is tilted to the right to expose the left colic flexure and its attachments. The phrenicocolic ligament is divided, releasing the left colonic flexure and exposing the inferior pole of the spleen.

The surgeon carefully dissects the splenocolic ligament, revealing the anatomical relations between the colon, small bowel, stomach, and spleen. Using a Maryland clamp, a small window is created around the inferior polar artery and vein, which are prepared for ligation with three Hemlock clips.

Two hemlock clips are placed on the remaining side of the inferior polar vein, and one on the specimen side. The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the renal splenic ligament, the posterior attachment of the spleen, aiming to create space for the upcoming dissection of the superior pole.

The surgeon dissects and ligates the inferior polar artery using blunt dissection to create a window around the vessel, applying three hemlock clips for ligation. The short gastric vessels are prepared, and the gastrosplenic ligament is divided using an energy device, which seals the vessels effectively. The anatomical proximity to the splenic hilum is considered throughout the procedure.

The surgeon divides part of the phrenicosplenic ligament and proceeds to the splenic hilum, where tortuous vessels are stapled whole to minimize bleeding risk. A Maryland clamp is used to create a passage for the stapling device, which is kept closed for thirty seconds to reduce post-stapler bleeding. The remaining gastrosplenic and phrenicosplenic ligaments are divided, and the spleen is extracted through a midline incision using a wound protector and endobag.
Histopathology confirms the diagnosis of a primary follicular lymphoma.
